title_en: Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani and the Completion of the Rijal Tradition
title_ar: ابن حجر العسقلاني وإتمام علم الرجال
content_en:
Lisan al-Mizan — The Tongue of the Balance — represents Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani's expansion and correction of adh-Dhahabi's Mizan al-I'tidal, and it stands as the culmination of the classical tradition of narrator-criticism dictionaries. Ibn Hajar, born in Cairo in 773 AH (1372 CE), was the greatest hadith scholar of the later Mamluk period and the most comprehensive of all the hadith biographers. His mastery of the entire narrator-criticism literature — from the earliest critics of the second Islamic century through adh-Dhahabi's generation — enabled him to produce a work that both preserved and surpassed the Mizan.

Ibn Hajar's relationship to adh-Dhahabi was that of a respectful student who had absorbed his predecessor's work but had gone further. He studied adh-Dhahabi's biographical works intensively and drew on them throughout his own scholarship. But he also found areas where adh-Dhahabi's coverage was incomplete or where his assessments could be refined, and Lisan al-Mizan was his systematic response to these gaps and corrections.

The scholarly context in which Lisan al-Mizan was produced was characterized by a commitment to preserving and systematizing the classical hadith tradition. The era of direct hadith transmission — when a scholar's chain of teachers led back to the Prophet through a short chain — was over, and the focus of hadith scholarship had shifted to the critical evaluation and commentary of the existing literature. Ibn Hajar's life work can be understood as a comprehensive effort to stabilize and certify the classical hadith tradition: through Fath al-Bari (commentary on al-Bukhari), through Tahdhib at-Tahdhib (evaluation of the narrators in the six canonical collections), and through Lisan al-Mizan (evaluation of criticized narrators in broader works).

Lisan al-Mizan was composed in the early part of Ibn Hajar's scholarly career, before Fath al-Bari, and it reflects the thorough grounding in the rijal literature that made his later works so authoritative.
